github.name: Cognitive-Core-Skills
github.description: A universal, industry-neutral taxonomy of cognitive core skills (perception, memory, reasoning, planning, action, verification, learning, governance) for LLMs, SLMs, AI agents, and world models — with schemas, 159 skill cards, benchmarks, and CI.

github.name: Awesome-Scientific-LLM-Benchmarks
github.description: A curated, accuracy-first list of benchmarks for evaluating LLMs on scientific reasoning and discovery — math, physics, chemistry, materials, biology, and agentic science.

github.name: qwen-lora-finetune
github.description: Fine-tune Qwen2.5-Coder for code generation on your own dataset using QLoRA. Train on JSONL instruction-response pairs and produce a shareable ~16 MB LoRA adapter.

github.name: mpesa_safaricom-fraud_anomaly_detection
github.description: Real-time fraud detection engine for M-Pesa mobile money — Kafka/Flink streaming, ML-based risk scoring, and explainable multi-signal checks (velocity, SIM swap correlation, mule networks) feeding an interaction-aware decision engine, with a full production pipeline from ingestion through dbt analytics.

github.name: Framegen
github.description: Real-time neural frame interpolation in the browser: hand-written WGSL runtime on raw WebGPU, 2.9 MB model, 2x-6x any <video> at 3-4 ms per frame. Chrome extension.

github.name: creatorhub
github.description: 多平台内容监控·采集·搬运 —— 纯 Python(FastAPI + Playwright),一个 Web 面板管起抖音 / 小红书 / 快手

github.name: fortress
github.description: Stealth Chromium engine that stops scrapers and browser agents from getting blocked, with one line of code change.

github.name: OmniPost-AI
github.description: AI-powered Chrome extension for generating and publishing posts to Facebook, Threads, and X using ChatGPT or Gemini.

github.name: a-fable-of-codexes
github.description: Claude Code skills that make Claude (Fable) the conductor of AI worker fleets: many parallel OpenAI Codex workers plus Opus design agents, organized into campaigns, waves, squads, and review gates.

trends: Today's tech landscape is dominated by the rapid evolution of AI, particularly in agentic systems and efficient model deployment. We're seeing a strong shift towards multi-agent orchestration frameworks where AIs collaborate to perform complex tasks, often incorporating built-in verification and reasoning capabilities to ensure reliability and explainability. Simultaneously, there's a significant focus on making AI more accessible and performant at the 'edge' – evidenced by the rise of small, specialized models and browser-based machine learning (WebGPU/WASM) that enable real-time, on-device processing. This efficiency drive, partly fueled by the 'AI margin collapse' discussion, also extends to practical, high-impact applications like real-time fraud detection and robust content automation. Developers are leveraging AI to create sophisticated productivity tools that automate multi-platform content generation, data collection, and even large-scale system setup, while also grappling with the need for resilient scraping techniques. Underlying these advancements is an increasing emphasis on defining and benchmarking fundamental AI cognitive skills, paving the way for more sophisticated and adaptable AI solutions in real-world, dynamic environments like robotics.

ideas.title: Agentic Multi-Platform Content Orchestrator
ideas.description: Develop an AI-powered system that acts as an orchestrator for content generation and multi-platform publishing. It will take a high-level content brief (e.g., 'promote a new feature for Project X on social media this week'), break it down into platform-specific tasks (e.g., LinkedIn post, Twitter thread, Instagram story concept), generate content drafts using a fine-tuned small LLM (e.g., Qwen2.5-Coder for specific tone/style), perform a 'self-verification' step (using an LLM-as-a-verifier logic) for tone, accuracy, and platform guidelines, and then schedule or directly publish the content via API integrations (e.g., Facebook Graph API, X API, Instagram API, etc.). It would feature a dashboard to monitor publishing success and content engagement.
tech_stack: LLM APIs (e.g., Qwen2.5-Coder via Hugging Face/LoRA, OpenAI/Gemini for verification)
ideas.source_inspiration: OmniPost-AI, creatorhub, a-fable-of-codexes, qwen-lora-finetune, LLM-as-a-Verifier, Cognitive-Core-Skills
steps: 1. Set up FastAPI backend with endpoints for content brief submission and publishing status.

2. Implement a multi-agent system: a 'Planner Agent' to break down the brief, a 'Generator Agent' (using fine-tuned LLM) for drafts, and a 'Verifier Agent' (using a more powerful LLM) for quality checks.
3. Integrate social media APIs for target platforms (e.g., Facebook, X, LinkedIn) for publishing and scheduling.
4. Develop a simple web UI for submitting briefs, monitoring agent progress, and reviewing/approving content.
5. Explore and implement QLoRA fine-tuning for Qwen2.5-Coder on a custom dataset of brand-specific social media content to improve generation quality.
6. Implement a task queue (Celery) for asynchronous content generation and publishing to handle long-running operations.

ideas.title: Real-time Browser-based AI Video Utility
ideas.description: Create a browser extension that enhances online video consumption using real-time, on-device AI. The primary feature would be dynamic frame interpolation (similar to Framegen) for smoother video playback on any HTML5 video element. As an advanced feature, integrate a small, browser-optimized embedding model (like Ternlight) to detect and highlight key moments or objects within the video in real-time, providing an interactive overlay. This could also include basic on-device summarization of video segments (e.g., 'action scene detected', 'dialogue-heavy part'). The goal is to offload computationally intensive AI tasks to the user's browser, leveraging WebGPU and WebAssembly for performance.
tech_stack: Pre-trained small ML models (e.g., frame interpolation model, object detection model converted for browser use)
ideas.source_inspiration: Framegen, Ternlight, Small AI Models Gain Traction In places with unreliable networks
steps: 1. Develop a basic browser extension (manifest, background script, content script) to inject functionality into video pages.
2. Research and integrate a WebGPU-optimized frame interpolation model to process video frames in real-time.
3. Implement the frame interpolation logic in a content script, rendering processed frames back into the video element.
4. Explore small embedding models (e.g., MobileNet SSD for object detection) suitable for WASM/TensorFlow.js inference.
5. Develop an interactive overlay using standard web technologies (HTML/CSS/JS) to display detected events or objects.
6. Optimize model loading and inference for minimal impact on browser performance, focusing on user experience.

ideas.title: Contextual Anomaly Explanation and Verification Workbench
ideas.description: Build a workbench that augments traditional anomaly detection systems (e.g., fraud, system errors, unusual user behavior) with an LLM-as-a-Verifier agent. When an anomaly is flagged, the system feeds relevant contextual data (e.g., transaction logs, user activity history, system metrics) to a specialized LLM. This LLM agent is tasked with generating a human-readable explanation for the anomaly, identifying potential root causes, suggesting next steps for investigation, and scoring the 'suspicion level' based on a predefined 'cognitive core skills' framework (e.g., reasoning, verification). A user interface would allow human operators to review these AI-generated insights, provide feedback, and confirm/reject anomalies, effectively creating a human-in-the-loop verification process to improve both AI and human analyst performance.
tech_stack: Vector Database (e.g., ChromaDB, Pinecone for context retrieval)
Stream processing (e.g., Apache Kafka / Redis Streams for simulated anomaly input)
Graph Database (Neo4j for relationship mapping if applicable)
Frontend (Dash/Streamlit for rapid prototyping or React/Vue.js)
steps: 1. Define a schema for anomaly alerts and associated contextual data (e.g., user ID, timestamps, event type, relevant logs).
2. Set up a data ingestion pipeline (simulated via Kafka/Redis or flat files) to receive anomaly alerts.
3. Implement a 'Context Retrieval' module to fetch relevant historical data based on the anomaly (e.g., from a database or vector store).
4. Design and implement the 'LLM-as-a-Verifier' agent: craft prompts to analyze context, generate explanations, identify root causes, and suggest actions.
5. Develop a web-based user interface for displaying anomalies, AI-generated explanations, and allowing human feedback/verification.
6. Implement a feedback loop mechanism to log human decisions and use this data to refine LLM prompts or even fine-tune smaller models for specific verification tasks over time.

quick_actions: Dedicate one hour this week to experiment with a small, open-source LLM (e.g., Qwen2.5-Coder) and try a basic QLoRA fine-tuning tutorial on a simple custom dataset.
Spend 30 minutes researching WebGPU and WebAssembly (WASM) for machine learning, focusing on how models can be deployed and run directly in the browser.
Build a minimal Python script leveraging an LLM API to simulate a multi-step agentic workflow – for instance, asking it to plan a simple project, then writing a short description for each step.

Schedule a recurring 'deep work' block (e.g., 60-90 minutes, 3 times a week) in your calendar specifically for focused learning on a new technology or tackling a complex coding challenge without interruptions.
